input.initialPrompt:
I'm setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11 from scratch. I need
to target both Android and Windows. I can't run commands right now — give
me a complete step-by-step plan with all the PowerShell commands I'll need,
including how to discover the right versions dynamically.

data.content:
Here's a complete plan for setting up .NET MAUI on Windows 11 (Android + Windows targets).

---

## Step 1 — Install the .NET SDK

Download the latest .NET SDK (MAUI requires .NET 8+):

```powershell
# Check if dotnet is already installed
dotnet --list-sdks

# Install via winget (preferred)
winget search Microsoft.DotNet.SDK
winget install Microsoft.DotNet.SDK.8 --accept-source-agreements

# Verify
dotnet --version
```

## Step 2 — Install the MAUI Workload

```powershell
# Discover available workloads
dotnet workload search maui

# Install MAUI (includes Android, iOS stubs, etc.)
dotnet workload install maui

# Verify installed workloads
dotnet workload list
```

## Step 3 — Install Visual Studio Build Tools (Windows target)

Windows target needs the Windows App SDK and WinUI tooling:

```powershell
# Option A: Full Visual Studio (recommended)
winget install Microsoft.VisualStudio.2022.Community --accept-source-agreements

# Then modify the installation to include these workloads:
#   - .NET Multi-platform App UI development
#   - Universal Windows Platform development
#   - .NET desktop development

# Option B: Command-line install with workloads
winget install Microsoft.VisualStudio.2022.Community `
  --override "--add Microsoft.VisualStudio.Workload.NetCrossPlat `
              --add Microsoft.VisualStudio.Workload.ManagedDesktop `
              --passive --wait"
```

## Step 4 — Set Up Android SDK & Emulator

MAUI ships Android SDK pieces, but you may need to manage them:

```powershell
# Install OpenJDK (required by Android tooling)
winget search Microsoft.OpenJDK
winget install Microsoft.OpenJDK.17

# Verify Java
java --version

# Find where MAUI installed the Android SDK
dotnet workload list
# Typical path: $env:LOCALAPPDATA\Microsoft\Android\sdk

# Set ANDROID_HOME if not already set
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable(
  "ANDROID_HOME",
  "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Microsoft\Android\sdk",
  "User"
)
$env:ANDROID_HOME = "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Microsoft\Android\sdk"

# Discover installed Android SDK components
& "$env:ANDROID_HOME\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --list

# Install/update required components dynamically
& "$env:ANDROID_HOME\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" `
  "platform-tools" `
  "platforms;android-34" `
  "build-tools;34.0.0" `
  "system-images;android-34;google_apis;x86_64"

# Accept all licenses
& "$env:ANDROID_HOME\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --licenses
```

## Step 5 — Create & Start an Android Emulator

```powershell
# List available device definitions
& "$env:ANDROID_HOME\emulator\emulator.exe" -list-avds
& "$env:ANDROID_HOME\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\avdmanager.bat" list device

# Create an AVD (pick a system image you installed above)
& "$env:ANDROID_HOME\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\avdmanager.bat" create avd `
  --name "Pixel_API_34" `
  --package "system-images;android-34;google_apis;x86_64" `
  --device "pixel_7"

# Launch the emulator
Start-Process "$env:ANDROID_HOME\emulator\emulator.exe" `
  -ArgumentList "-avd Pixel_API_34"
```

## Step 6 — Enable Windows Developer Mode

Required for deploying unpackaged Windows apps:

```powershell
# Enable Developer Mode via registry
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AppModelUnlock" `
  /v AllowDevelopmentWithoutDevLicense /t REG_DWORD /d 1 /f
```

## Step 7 — Enable Hyper-V (Android Emulator acceleration)

```powershell
# Run as Administrator
Enable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online `
  -FeatureName Microsoft-Hyper-V-All -NoRestart

# Also enable Windows Hypervisor Platform
Enable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online `
  -FeatureName HypervisorPlatform -NoRestart

# Restart required
Restart-Computer
```

## Step 8 — Scaffold & Verify a Project

```powershell
# List available MAUI templates
dotnet new list maui

# Create a new MAUI app
dotnet new maui -n MyMauiApp
cd MyMauiApp

# Build for Windows (WinUI)
dotnet build -f net8.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Build for Android
dotnet build -f net8.0-android

# Run on Windows
dotnet run -f net8.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Run on Android emulator (emulator must be running)
dotnet build -f net8.0-android -t:Install -t:Run
```

## Step 9 — Discover Versions Dynamically

Useful if you revisit this later and need current values:

```powershell
# Latest .NET SDK available via winget
winget search Microsoft.DotNet.SDK

# Installed MAUI workload versions
dotnet workload list --versions

# Available target frameworks
dotnet --list-sdks
dotnet new maui --dry-run  # shows target framework it would use

# Available Android SDK platforms
& "$env:ANDROID_HOME\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --list 2>&1 |
  Select-String "^platforms"

# Available Windows SDK versions (for -f flag)
Get-ChildItem "C:\Program Files (x86)\Windows Kits\10\Include" |
  Select-Object Name
```

---

**Key gotchas:**
- Run VS Installer and Android SDK commands as **Administrator**.
- After workload/feature installs, **restart your terminal** so `$env:PATH` picks up changes.
- The `net8.0-windows10.0.19041.0` TFM must match an installed Windows SDK — use Step 9 to discover what's on your machine.
- If `dotnet workload install maui` fails behind a proxy, set `$env:HTTPS_PROXY` first.
data.reasoningText: The user wants a complete step-by-step plan for setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11, targeting Android and Windows. They can't run commands now, so they want all the PowerShell commands listed out. Let me provide a comprehensive guide.
